finding: A severe intervention option produced a nonmonotonic (U-shaped) effect on aggregate welfare under imperfect information; the costliest intervention reached welfare parity with no intervention available, whereas moderate costs degraded welfare below the no-intervention baseline.

Control authority had nonpecuniary utility value (individuals retained control despite material loss), and authority possession increased effort provision by controllers but misaligned effort in subordinates, amplifying inefficiency.
A sequential selection procedure produced outcomes consistent with theory and outperformed a simultaneous procedure in addressing selection quality under bilateral participation.
Agents coordinated on a less efficient system-state despite availability of more efficient alternatives; the system-state functioned as a coordination device that trumped Pareto-superior options.

A high-magnitude cost subsidy dramatically increased access but created substantial off-target consumption; two targeting strategies (reduced subsidy magnitude and diagnostic testing) differentially addressed the access-accuracy trade-off.

An observability cue suppressed single women's costly self-presentation but not other groups; the effect was moderated by observer gender (single male peers), suggesting social-signaling constraints on behavior.

Exposure to an external system-context via migration shifted political outcomes and preferences in the origin community; differential exposure (to high versus low system-contexts) provided quasi-experimental leverage.
An individual difference (receptiveness trait) predicted information-consumption diversity, attentional engagement with opposing arguments, and balanced evaluation of competing claims.

International resource access enabled market internationalization, with this association moderated by temporal and organizational-affiliation context; market internationalization subsequently affected financial performance.
A reference-dependent heuristic (anchoring to comparison item) caused distorted estimates of a target item's property; the effect was independent of mediating property-perception shifts.
Heterogeneous individual differences in reasoning depth were present and improved model fit over homogeneous-population assumptions; this pattern was stronger in comprehension than production.

Individuals adjusted unethical behavior to extract benefit while preserving self-perception; two cognitive mechanisms (inattention to standards and categorization flexibility) enabled this self-concept maintenance.
A contextual cue (table-surface material) produced opposite-direction effects on target evaluations depending on a cognitive mode (holistic versus analytic cognition) primed by self-view.

A social function of target preference (adjustment-oriented versus value-expressive) predicted preference for an unauthorized copy and spill-over devaluation of the authorized option; moral beliefs moderated the latter when value-expressive.

Branding shifted the function associated with a product category (utilitarian categories became more symbolic; symbolic became more utilitarian), eliminating the function-matching persuasion advantage observed at the category level.

Delegating a low-effort decision to a surrogate depleted self-regulatory resources more than autonomous decision-making; this effect required only the memory or prospect of delegation and was reversed by reaffirming belief in autonomy.

A linguistic frame (risk-associated label versus neutral label) modulated willingness-to-pay but not willingness-to-accept; buyers and sellers weighted outcome information asymmetrically, a difference mediated by risk-aversion in acquisition versus risk-neutrality in relinquishment.

A static visual cue evoked an implicit property (dynamic imagery), which increased engagement with the cue; engagement subsequently raised attitudes, with a moderation by congruence between the implied property and target attributes.

Salience of a situational constraint (scarcity) polarized item evaluations within a set and increased preference for the most-preferred item; arousal mediated the effect and was itself modulated by induced arousal state.

Perceived conflict among personal goals predicted subjective time scarcity beyond goal quantity; stress and anxiety mediated this effect, and two interventions (slow breathing and anxiety reframing) reduced the scarcity perception.
